title: Tax and superannuation
content:
Tax and superannuation

Our role:
The FWO provides information and advice on workplace rights and obligations, including the entitlement to superannuation (super) in the NES and extra terms about super in awards. The ATO is the PRIMARY enforcement agency for the compulsory super guarantee.

Superannuation guarantee:
Under the super guarantee, employers MUST pay super contributions of 12% of an employee's ordinary time earnings (OTE) when an employee is:
- Over 18 years, OR
- Under 18 years and works over 30 hours a week

The super guarantee applies to ALL types of employees:
- Full-time employees
- Part-time employees
- Casual employees
- Temporary residents

Payment requirements:
- Employers must pay super contributions at LEAST every 3 months
- Must be paid into the employee's nominated super account
- If super is not paid on time, ADDITIONAL CHARGES apply
- Late payment may also BREACH the Fair Work Act or applicable award/EA

PAYDAY SUPER — NEW RULES FROM 1 JULY 2026:
Starting 1 July 2026, employers will need to pay superannuation contributions AT THE SAME TIME they pay their employees' wages.

Super under the NES:
Super is an entitlement under the NES. Most employees covered by the NES can take court action under the Fair Work Act to recover unpaid super, UNLESS the ATO has already started proceedings.

The NES entitlement to super aligns with super laws — if an employer complies with the super guarantee, they also meet NES obligations.

NES super entitlement does NOT apply to some employees — broadly, employees in NSW, SA, QLD, TAS and VIC who are employed by sole traders, partnerships, other unincorporated entities, or non-trading corporations. These employees may still be entitled to super under the super guarantee and award/agreement terms.

Extra super terms in awards:
Some awards, enterprise agreements and other registered agreements have extra terms about super beyond the super guarantee. These may specify:
- Employer contributions to specific industry super funds
- Additional contribution rates
- Fund choice restrictions

When super hasn't been paid:
Employees who think super hasn't been paid can make a complaint to the ATO. They should:
1. Check if an award or registered agreement applies
2. Confirm if the award/agreement has extra super terms
3. Follow steps on ATO - Unpaid super from your employer page

If there are extra super terms in an award/agreement, employees can also contact the FWO.

Current super rate: 12% of OTE (ordinary time earnings)

Source: Fair Work Act 2009 sections 116A-116E
